coredata.dc:title: “Carbon quantum dots-glue” enabled high-capacitance and highly stable nickel sulphide nanosheet electrode for supercapacitors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Fangya
Shao, Lianyi
Shi, Xiaoyan
Wu, Fangdan
Huang, Haitao
Sun, Zhipeng
Trukhanov, Alex
coredata.dc:description: A facile “carbon quantum dots glue” strategy for the fabrication of honeycomb-like carbon quantum dots/nickel sulphide network arrays on Ni foam surface is successfully demonstrated. This design realizes the immobilization of nanosheet arrays and maintains a strong adhesion to the collector, forming a three-dimensional (3D) honeycomb-like architecture. Thanks to the unique structural advantages, the resulting bind-free electrode with high active mass loading of 6.16 mg cm−2 still exhibits a superior specific capacitance of 1130F g−1 at 2 A g−1, and maintains 80% of the initial capacitance even at 10 A g−1 after 3000 cycles. Furthermore, the assembled asymmetrical supercapacitor delivers an energy density of 18.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 134 W kg−1, and outstanding cycling stability (100% of initial capacitance retention after 5000 cycles at 5 mA cm−2). These impressive results indicate a new perspective to design various binder-free electrodes for electrochemical energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nickel sulphide
Carbon quantum dots
Honeycomb-like network arrays
Binder-free electrode
Supercapacitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the Ni3S2 lattice structure, the continuous Ni − Ni paths have been highlighted as green bonds. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
SEM images of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF (a, b, c) and Ni3S2/NF (d, e, f).
a) TEM image of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF. b) TEM image of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF and the corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern.
a) Experimental XRD pattern of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF and Ni3S2/NF. b) The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm plot of CQDs@Ni3S2. The inset shows pore distribution of CQDs@Ni3S2.
a) XPS spectra of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF survey, b) C 1 s, c) S 2p, d) Ni 2p.
a) Cyclic voltammograms of CQDs/Ni3S2@NF at different scan rates. b) Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of the CQDs/Ni3S2@NF at different current densities.

a) Cyclic voltammograms of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF and Ni3S2/NF at 20 mVs−1. b) Specific capacitance as a function of current density of the CQDs/Ni3S2/NF and Ni3S2/NF. c) Nyquist plot of the electrodes composed of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF and Ni3S2/NF, the inset shows the expanded high-frequency region of the plots. d) Cycling performance of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF at 10 A g−1, the inset shows the expanded high-frequency region of the plots.

a) CV curves of the AC electrode and CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode collected at 20 mv s−1. b) CV curves of the CQDs/Ni3S2/NF@AC ASC device collected for varying potential ranges at a scan rate of 20 mv s−1. c) CV curves of the ASC device at different scan rates. d) GCD curves of ASC device at different current density. e) Ragone plot of the ASC device compared with other reported ASC results. f) Cycling performance of the ASC device at a current density 5 mA cm−2. The inset shows the first and last 5 cycles of charge/discharge curves of ASC.

head.ce:title: “Carbon quantum dots-glue” enabled high-capacitance and highly stable nickel sulphide nanosheet electrode for supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Kowloon, Hong Kong, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Materials Centre, SSPA “Scientific and Practical Materials Research Centre of NAS of Belarus”, 19 P. Brovki St, 220072 Minsk, Belarus

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Functional Materials Centre, SSPA “Scientific and Practical Materials Research Centre of NAS of Belarus”

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Functional Materials Centre, SSPA “Scientific and Practical Materials Research Centre of NAS of Belarus”, 19 P. Brovki St, 220072 Minsk, Belarus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology R&E Centre, South Ural State University, 76, Lenina ave, Chelyabinsk 454080, Russia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology R&E Centre, South Ural State University, 76, Lenina ave, Chelyabinsk 454080, Russia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ni3S2/CQDs with 3D honeycomb-like architecture are prepared on Ni foam surface, demonstrating outstanding electrochemical properties for supercapacitors.

A facile “carbon quantum dots glue” strategy for the fabrication of honeycomb-like carbon quantum dots/nickel sulphide network arrays on Ni foam surface is successfully demonstrated. This design realizes the immobilization of nanosheet arrays and maintains a strong adhesion to the collector, forming a three-dimensional (3D) honeycomb-like architecture. Thanks to the unique structural advantages, the resulting bind-free electrode with high active mass loading of 6.16 mg cm−2 still exhibits a superior specific capacitance of 1130F g−1 at 2 A g−1, and maintains 80% of the initial capacitance even at 10 A g−1 after 3000 cycles. Furthermore, the assembled asymmetrical supercapacitor delivers an energy density of 18.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 134 W kg−1, and outstanding cycling stability (100% of initial capacitance retention after 5000 cycles at 5 mA cm−2). These impressive results indicate a new perspective to design various binder-free electrodes for electrochemical energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Developing advanced energy storage devices has become a hot topic due to the rising demand in modern portable electronics and environmental protection [1,2]. Supercapacitors (SCs) have received abundant attention because of their fast charging/discharging rate, high power density, and excellent lifespan [3,4]. Unfortunately, the practical widespread application of SCs is still partially hampered by its low energy density. To meet the demands for advanced SCs, it is considerable to design and fabricate active materials with desired electrochemical performances [5]. Currently, carbon, conductive polymers, metal oxides, and metal sulphides are being extensively studied as promising electrode materials for SCs [6,7]. However, carbon often shows poor capacitive performances, which is owning to their limited reversibility of ion absorption on the electrolyte/electrode interface [8–10]. Conducting polymers typically exhibit unsatisfied cyclic life during long-time charge/discharge test [11,12]. Metal oxides with relatively low conductivity often result in mediocre rate capability [13–18]. Especially, metal sulphides including NiCo2S4, MoS2, CuCo2S4, CoS, and Ni3S2, are well known as an emerging class of pseudocapacitive materials for SCs [19–29]. Particularly, nickel sulphide (Ni3S2) is one of notable promising transition metal sulphides for energy storage due to its intriguing low diffusion resistance to protons/cations, good electronic conductivity, and excellent electrolyte penetration ability [30–31]. It should be noted that each Ni atom forms four Ni − Ni bonds in the structure of heazlewoodite Ni3S2 (Fig. 1 ), whereas Ni atoms are connected along highly these Ni − Ni bonds, and these bond distances are short and similar to those in Ni metal, therefore resulting in its better conductivity compared to other nickel-based compounds [32]. From now on, multiple types of Ni3S2 nanostructures, such as hollow spheres, [33] nanowires [34] and nanoflakes, [35] have been reported and evaluated in terms of their electrochemical performances. These studies have made great progresses. However, considering the electrode with high mass loading (≥4 mg cm−2), several tough problems such as unsatisfying capacitance, poor cycling stability are still limited their actual applications. One of the possible reasons is that the introduction of additives, conductive materials and binders during the preparation process of the electrode. This will inevitably introduce extra contact resistance and sacrifice the overall energy storage capacity [13,25]. As a new direction, 3D porous electrode has recently emerged, which can both provide a highly electronic conductive framework without conductive phases and polymer binder, and possess a high surface area and hierarchical porous nanostructure favouring facile faradic reaction and fast electrolyte diffusion [15,28,32,36]. Many published researches have already focused on the optimization of morphology and incorporating Ni3S2 with conductive materials to obtain excellent electrochemical performance [37]. For example, Ni3S2@Ni(OH)2@3DGN/Ni foam displayed a capacitance of 1037.5F g−1 at 5.1 A g−1 [38]. Ni3S2-rGO composites supported on Ni foam presented a capacitance of 1886F g−1 at 1 A g−1 [39]. Ni3S2/carbon nanofibers showed a capacitance of 883F g−1 at 2 A g−1 [40]. Ni3S2 nanoparticles deposited on MWCNTs exhibited a capacitance of 800F g−1 at 3.2 A g−1 [41]. However, because the targeted electroactive materials with high mass loading may suffer from some issues, such as incompatibility with 3D conductive scaffold, low-rate capability and poor electrochemical stability, which still remain an important challenging for the direct grown it on 3D conductive scaffold.

As one type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the diameters of about 1–2 nm have good conductivity and high surface area. Moreover, CQDs possess extensive polar groups, which is of benefit to improving surface wettability and forming larger interface areas among CQDs, electrolytes and transition metal oxides for reactions, which can facilitate and improve the electron transportation in the electrode [26,27]. Most importantly, CQDs are more flexible for engineering multifarious nanostructures than those multidimensional carbon materials. For example, wang et al. fabricated N-doped carbon dots/NiCo2O4 composites with various morphologies, delivering a great specific capacitance of 800F g−1 at 3.2 A g−1 [28]. All these indicate that Ni3S2-CQDs composites with novel nanostructure are suitable for potential application in the field of electrochemical energy storage.

In this work, a meaningful “carbon quantum dots glue” penetration strategy for Ni3S2 nanosheet arrays has been proposed. We have successfully designed and synthesized honeycomb-like CQDs/Ni3S2 nanosheet arrays on the Ni foam (NF) as bind-free electrode directly. For comparison, both Ni3S2 nanostructure with and without CQDs modified are prepared via hydrothermal method on a NF substrate (denoted as CQDs/Ni3S2@NF and Ni3S2@NF), respectively. The electrochemical results reveal that CQDs/Ni3S2/NF possesses much enhanced capacitive properties compared with pure phase Ni3S2/NF. Moreover, the assembled asymmetrical supercapacitor delivers an energy density of 18.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 134 W kg−1, and outstanding cycling stability (100% of initial capacitance retention after 5000 cycles at 5 mA cm−2). These results indicate that CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode with high active mass loading has a significant potential in advanced energy storage devices.

Ni foam (the diameter is 0.1 mm, the thickness is 1.0 mm) was purchased from Changsha Lyrun new material Co. Ltd. Carbon quantum dots aqueous solution (1 mg·mL−1) was purchased from XFNANO Co. Ltd. Other chemical reagents in this experiment were all analytically pure. At the beginning, a section of Ni foam (1 × 4 cm2) was washed by ultrasonicator using acetone, 6.0 M HCl, distilled water, and ethanol for 20 min, respectively. Then treated Ni foam was dried and weighed to obtain the pre-loading weigh M0.

Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (5 mmol) and Urotropine (10 mmol) were mixed together with 35 mL carbon quantum dots aqueous solution (1 mg·mL−1) and stirred vigorously at 30 °C for 10 min. The NF (M0 = 123.02 mg) prepared in advance was soaked into above solution and then strongly sonicated for 30 min . Then they were transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and maintained 100 °C for 10 h. The resulted material was washed with extensive water and allocated to another autoclave together with 30 mL sodium sulphide (0.1 M) aqueous solution and kept at 120 °C for 10 h. The obtained material was dried at 60 °C and weighed to achieve the post-loading weigh (M1 = 147.66 mg, loading M = 24.64 mg).

Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (5 mmol) and Urotropine (10 mmol) were dissolved in 35 mL deionized water, the above mixture was kept at 30 °C and stirred for 10 min. Then NF (M0 = 123.11 mg) prepared in advance was put into the solution and strongly sonicated for 30 min. Subsequently, they were transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and kept at 100 °C for 10 h. The resulted material was washed with extensive water and allocated to another autoclave together with 30 mL sodium sulphide (0.1 M) aqueous solution and kept at 120 °C for 10 h. The obtained material was dried at 60 °C and weighed to achieve the post-loading weigh (M1′ = 140.65 mg, loading M’ = 17.54 mg).

The diffraction patterns of the obtained materials were performed with powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) techniques (Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.5418 Å). Morphologies and structures were investigated by a scanning electron microscopy (SEM, HITACHI Hitachi-4800 microscope) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010FEF). Information of elemental compositions and binding energies was characterised with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and a Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD spectrometer. The UV–vis absorption spectra was characterised by a Thermofisher UV–vis spectrophotometer.

All electrochemical tests were measured with CHI 660E electrochemical workstation in 1 M KOH aqueous solution. For three-electrode system measurement, a Pt foil, a standard calomel electrode (SCE) and as-prepared CQDs/Ni3S2/NF was utilized as the counter electrode, reference electrode and working electrode, respectively. The specific capacitances were calculated using the equations [42]:
C =
It
mV
, where I (mA) is the discharge current, t(s) is the discharge time, m(mg) is the mass loading of active materials, and V(V) is the window of potential voltage.

The morphology feature of the CQDs is shown by TEM image. It can be observed from Figure S1 that CQDs have a narrow size distribution and present a mean lateral diameter of 2.3 nm. In addition, it is shown form Figure S2 that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQDs shows a peak at ca. 350 nm, which is attributed to the electron transitions from the π(or n) to π * orbital of CO bond. And the CQD dispersion appears light-brown fluorescence under UV (365 nm). Furthermore, CQDs/Ni3S2 and pure Ni3S2 are synthesized by a straightforward hydrothermal synthesis utilizing NF as support. The NF as the substrate provides high specific surface area for the in-situ growth of Ni3S2. Complete coverage on the NF surface with honeycomb-like Ni3S2 nanosheets is observed in Fig. 2 a and d. Meanwhile, both the CQDs/Ni3S2 (Fig. 2a, b, and c) and Ni3S2 (Fig. 2d, e, and f) display a highly loose and porous morphology with a honeycomb-like nanostructure on NF. According to EDS-mapping, it can be seen that Ni and S elements are evenly distributed on the surface of nickel foam and a small amount of carbon element exists (Figure S3). It is noteworthy that the CQDs/Ni3S2/NF appears to be finer compared with Ni3S2/NF, therefore, indicating that such architectures may provide high active surface area and ensure continuous ion/electron transportation pathways. Furthermore, the TEM images of CQDs/Ni3S2 shows a thin multiple crumpled nanosheets (Fig. 3 a), which is agree with the result of SEM analysis. And its nanocrystalline phase can be detected in the high-resolution TEM and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 3b). The lattice fringe with regular spacing of 0.408, 0.280, and 0.194 nm is associated with the (1 0 1), (2–1 0), and (3–1 1) planes of Ni3S2, respectively. Meanwhile, the graphitized lattice of CQDs with spacing of 0.345 nm is also detected in the CQDs/Ni3S2/NF composites [28] indicating the COQs have been infiltrated into the interspacing of individual nanosheets. Moreover, the crystallinity of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF is further confirmed by XRD (Fig. 4 a, green). The patterns well correspond to Ni3S2 (JCPDS No. 44–1418, space group R32; a = 4.046, b = 8.090, c = 4.047 Å). In addition, the Ni3S2/NF shows the same crystallinity as CQDs/Ni3S2/NF as indicated by the XRD data (Fig. 4a, black). Fig. 4b shows the nitrogen adsorption desorption plots of CQDs/Ni3S2. The test data indicates that this material has a largest specific surface area (Sa) of 59.99 m2 g−1 and pore volume of 0.192 cm3 g−1. Additionally, the average pore size distribution concentrates around 3–10 nm, implying the existence of mesopores in CQDs/Ni3S2.

The binding energies in CQDs/Ni3S2/NF were characterized by XPS. As observed in Fig. 5 a, the as-prepared CQDs/Ni3S2/NF is composed of three main elements of Ni, C and S. The XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 5b) is divided into three peaks, which is typical signal of CC (284.6 eV), CO (286.7 eV), and CO (288.6 eV), respectively, indicating the existence of CQDs in CQDs/Ni3S2/NF. Meanwhile, the S 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 binding energy is 161.8 eV and 168.0 eV (Fig. 5c, fitted line in purple and green), respectively [43]. Apparently, the binding energies for element Ni are spotted as two strong major peaks locate at 855.6 eV and 872.5 eV accompanied by the satellite peaks, which is assigned to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, respectively. Importantly, the energy difference between Ni 2p3/2 (861.6 eV) and Ni 2p1/2 (879.2 eV) is observed to be 17.6 eV, implying the coexistence of Ni2+ and Ni3+, which is characteristic of a Ni3S2 phase [44–49].

The electrochemical performance of the honeycomb-like nanostructure CQDs/Ni3S2/NF was studied by cycle voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge/discharge techniques. Fig. 6 a shows that the shape of all CV curves of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode maintain intact and the current increases correspondingly with the increasing scan rates from 10 to 100 mV s−1. An apparent pair of redox peaks still can be distinctly recognized even at the scan rate of 100 mV s−1, suggesting that the optimized structure of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF is beneficial to fast redox reactions [33,38]. Interestingly, the redox peaks may be corresponded to the reversible transition between Ni(II) and Ni(III) as shown in equation (1) [34,39].
(1) Ni3S2 + 3OH− ↔ Ni3S2(OH)3 + 3e−

Additionally, while the scan rates increase from 10 to 100 mVs−1, the potential differences between anodic and cathodic peaks become larger. Only 75 mV shift for the redox peak potential is observed for 10 times increasing in the scan rates, this can be owning to excellent conductivity resulted low polarization of the electrode. Thus, high-power characteristic CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode can be expected since it is in favor of fast redox reactions [50]. As displayed in Figure S4, the current density shows a significant ratio relationship with the increase of scan rate, and the b value of oxidation peak is about 0.58 for CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode, indicating a fast kinetic reaction process. Besides, its collected charge/discharge curves at all tested current densities showing nearly symmetrical triangle shapes further evaluates the electrochemical performance (Fig. 6b). The calculated specific capacitances of the electrode (mloading = 6.16 mg cm−2) are about 1130 and 915F g−1 at 2 and 4 A g−1, respectively. More importantly, the specific capacitance of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode remains at 605F g−1 at a high current density of 15 A g−1, implying that it is also suitable for high current charge/discharge.

To compare the capacitive properties of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode and Ni3S2/NF electrode, their CV curves are obtained at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1. As can be seen in Fig. 7 a, the CV curve of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF exhibits much higher current as well as enclosed area than Ni3S2/NF electrode, indicating enhanced electrochemical properties (e.g., improved conductivity, reduced polarization). As a comparison group, charge and discharge performance of Ni3S2/NF at different current density is showed in Figure S5. Specific capacitance of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF and Ni3S2/NF electrode are showed in Fig. 7b. As can be shown, the CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode exhibits excellent capacitances of 1130, 915, 825, 750 and 605F g−1 at current densities of 2, 4, 6, 10 and 15 A g−1, respectively, which are higher than those of the Ni3S2/NF (980, 890, 760, 590 and 440F g−1, respectively). Especially, CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode also shows better columbic efficiency (~91.3%) than Ni3S2/NF electrode (~90.2%) for 2A g−1 charge/discharge profile. In addition, the Nyquist plots of the CQDs/Ni3S2/NF and Ni3S2/NF with a characteristic of semicircles and straight sloping lines in the high and low frequency region, respectively (Fig. 7c). From the Nyquist plots (inset of Fig. 7c), the solution resistances (Rs) of electrode increase slightly from 0.42 Ωto 0.45 Ωfor CQDs/Ni3S2/NF and Ni3S2/NF in the high frequency region, which represents the minimal resistance related to the ionic conductivity and electronic conductivity of the electrodes [28]. Moreover, the semicircle region also displays the charge transfer resistance, revealing the nature of the material and combination between the material and the current collector [6,32]. The semicircular diameter of Ni3S2/NF electrode is obviously larger than that of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode, which maybe originate from the slow transfer rate of ions of Ni3S2/NF. As we all know, a vertical line is expected in the Nyquist plots at the low frequency region for an ideal electrode, and the verticality of the line represents capacitive features of the electrode [51–53]. According to fitting by equivalent circuit diagram for Nyquis plots, Rs of two electrodes are about 0.4 Ω. Those result shows that CQDs/Ni3S2/NF and Ni3S2/NF electrode both exhibit great electrical conductivity [54]. Notably, a smaller slope of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode plot can be clearly seen compared to that of the Ni3S2/NF electrode, suggesting an enhanced performance of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode (Fig. 7c) [55]. It is clearly indicated that CQDs produce a valid effect on reducing the electrochemical impedance of the electrode materials. According to the Bode plots of the phase angle versus frequency of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode in Figure S6, the characteristic frequency (f 0) of 7.0 Hz at a phase of −45° and relaxation time constant (τ 0) of 143 ms are obtained, indicating high speed ion diffusion behaviour of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF. The long-term cycling performance of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode at a current density of 10 A g−1 is shown in Fig. 7d. As can be seen, the specific capacitance of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode still maintains at 615F g−1 after 3000 charge/discharge cycles, only a ca. 20% drop from the maximum value (770F g−1) even at high current density 10 A g−1. Form Figure S7, CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode also reveals the better wettability than Ni3S2/NF electrode in KOH electrolyte. Therefore, it may be inferred that the CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode is evidently superior to Ni3S2/NF electrodes, which may be facilitated by the low electrochemical impedance and good wettability. Based on the above results, such remarkable pseudocapacitive performance of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF might benefit from its unique nanostructure. First, the high surface area and mesoporous texture of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF provide many active sites for electrochemical reactions [11,38]. Second, the open space between the mesopores existing in these nanosheets may act as a robust reservoir for ions, and also obviously improve the diffusion kinetics within the electrode. Such hierarchical porous channels ensure the fast penetration of electrolyte even at high currents [29,41,44]. Third, each electroactive nanosheet with favorable intrinsic electrical conductivity directly contacts with Ni foam substrate, which may provide an express path for fast electron transport [34,42,49]. Finally, the structural features are beneficial to suitably relieve the nanostructure damage and availably relax the volume expansion during cycling, which is a result from the homogeneous distribution of CQDs in electrode materials [28,37]. Therefore, it is incontestable that CQDs make a significant contribution to the electrochemical performances of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode.

An asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) using activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode and CQDs/Ni3S2/NF as positive electrode is assembled in 6 M KOH. The CV curves of positive and negative electrodes are showed in Fig. 8 a. According to the Formula
m +
m -
=
C -
V -
C +
V +
, the theoretical mass of rate of positive and negative is 0.143. Fig. 8b displays the CV curves at different window of potential for ASC. Significant polarization will occur when the potential exceeds 1.5 V. Fig. 8c exhibits CV curves of ASC in different scan rate from 10 to 100 mv s−1, revealing the obvious double characteristics of electric double layer capacitance and pseudocapacitance in ASC. As shown in Fig. 8d, the specific capacity of ASC is about 0.34F cm−2 in the current density of 1 mA cm−2. The electrode mass of ASC is sum of the actual positive and negative material. The power density and energy density of ASC are calculated by formula
E =
C
V
2
7200
and
P =
3600 E
t
. This ASC displays a very high energy density of 18.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 134 W kg−1 and high power density of 1678 W kg−1 at energy density of 4.5 Wh kg−1 just like Fig. 8e, which exhibits outstanding more advantages than other reported ASC results, such as Ni3S2//Pen ink (8.2 Wh kg−1 at 214.6 W kg−1) [56] Ni3S2//AC (10 Wh kg−1 at 150 W kg−1) [57] NiO//AC (10.5 Wh kg−1 at 50 W kg−1) [58] Ni-Co Oxide//AC (12 Wh kg−1 at 95.2 W kg−1) [59] and MnFe2O4@C//LiMn2O4 (7.5 Wh kg−1 at 30 W kg−1) [60]. As shown in Fig. 8f, the long-term stability of ASC is also evaluated at current density of 5 mA cm−2, which indicates no obvious change in catalytic performance after 5000 cycles, indicating the outstanding cycling stability of CQDs/Ni3S2/NF//AC.

In summary, we design a “carbon quantum dots-glue” engineered Ni3S2 nanosheets/NF through a simple fabrication process and demonstrate its potential to be directly used as electrode material with high active mass loading for SCs. Attributed to the unique architecture, CQDs/Ni3S2/NF electrode with high active mass loading (6.16 mg cm−2) presents superior specific capacitance of 1130F g−1 at 2 A g−1, and maintain 80% of the initial capacitance even at 10 A g−1 after 3000 charge/discharge cycles in 1 M KOH solution. In addition, the ASC exhibits an energy density of 18.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 134 W kg−1, and outstanding cycling stability (100% of initial capacitance retention after 5000 cycles at 5 mA cm−2). This impressive work paves a new way to fabricate hierarchical metal sulfide/CQDs materials for high-performance energy devices.
Fangya Qi: Methodology, Data curation. Lianyi Shao: . Xiaoyan Shi: . Fangdan Wu: Writing - original draft. Haitao Huang: . Zhipeng Sun: . Alex Trukhanov: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21663029), Department of Science and Technology of Guangdong Province (No. 2020A1414010280).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.05.099.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interfacial engineering of nickel boride/metaborate and its effect on high energy density asymmetric supercapacitors
ACS Nano.
